My question: wouldn't MRI research be entirely impossible if we all actually strictly followed patent/ip law? We are publishing papers all the time that are based off previous papers on methods that are also patented. How do we ensure any sort of reproducibility in this environment?
column5: This is exactly the problem. In Europe, however, due to the research exception, methodological research is perfectly permissible. The US situation is little bit ugly though, at least from the formal legal point-of-view. But in practise, IP owners often chose to ignore such minor infrigement cases, such as scientific papers...

column2: I work in the field of quantitative MRI and have some of my codes in a github account, open for all. But now I am scared if I am unknowingly infringing on some patents like Synthetic MR and so on. Can I assume that if I am able to publish the work as a journal article, can I be sure that my codes are not infringing on any patents?
column5: don't be scared. it is basically the task of the IP owner to prove that your code infringes in a patent, so unless nobody goes for effort to do so, you are fine.

column2: I work in the field of quantitative MRI and have some of my codes in a github account, open for all. But now I am scared if I am unknowingly infringing on some patents like Synthetic MR and so on. Can I assume that if I am able to publish the work as a journal article, can I be sure that my codes are not infringing on any patents?
column5: I agree. since it takes a lot of money to enforce IP (i.e. hire lawyers to sue), patent holders are really only focused on enforcement of commercial uses. Open-source code for research/academic use are not the main targets here. If you try to start a company with the technology, then you need to consider if any of your product is patent-encumbered (and generally there are corporate lawyers and consultants who you can work through this process with).

column2: Is c2p agreement under the licence?
column5: no, C2P agreement is not a license

column2: Is GPL-2 less viral?  Your opinion?
column5: GPL2 is certainly not less viral, but it is more permissive in terms of the commercial use. But still, if you don't want to introduce constraints for the dissemination of your technology, then try to avoid GPL for your own work.

column2: Could you comment on the current license for pulseq matlab/python, and how we should think about out sequences built on top
column5: Cuttent Matlab-Pulseq and PyPulseq licensed under MIT, ther are no restrictions for derived works.

column2: I saw that pypulseq change the license and had to remove some code ? what about rewritting it ?
column5: the code we removed was not worth rewriting, as it was mostly unused or superseded (e.g., SAR prediction)

column2:
If a technology is patented, does that mean I cannot use it for my own research or publish a paper based on it?
For example, MRF is patented, would developing a method based on it and publishing a paper be considered patent infringement? but,, many researchers are already using MRF in their work.
column5: Strictly speaking in the US -- yes, it may be considered as an infringement. In Europe you are acvtually completely fine. There are many strange things about the US legal system, this is not the strangest one, but certainly one that worth mentioning.

column2: How do you deal with deciding on using libraries or dependancies in your software, based on their license? e.g. if you use a library that has GPL-2 or GPL-3, your software becomes encumbered?
column5: typically, if you use a GPL-based library, your software is not automatically subject to GPL,at least not its source code. But once you package a binary differences between GPL2 and GPL3 or AGPL start to be important...

column2: If we have code using pulseq, should we keep it off github? Even if it has not been published in a paper?
column5: of course not, please share your code! but bst make sure that your PI knows and proves that you share the code, and then it is the responsibility of the lab or the University and not of you personally.

column2: can MRzero be  used to simulate seq files for other body parts eg. liver
column5: yes, but you would probably need to define your own liver numerical phantom. there is instructions to write your own digital phantom online: https://mrsources.github.io/MRzero-Core/

column2: As a new pulseq user, I find myself writing custom trapezoid functions beyond the few provided in the library. However I am concerned that I could be unknowingly infringing on someone’s IP here. I expect this kind of thing comes up a lot for pulseq users who are “reinventing the wheel” in small ways as they create their own pulse sequence “from scratch”.  What is recommended for protecting ourselves, the pulseq sequence author who does not hold the IP, in this regard? Not share the pulseq code?
column5: Don't worry so much, most of the basic MR patents such as slice refocusing gradient or alike, have ran out by now, so they are in the public domain. So, don't be afraid, share your code and see what happens. To be on the safe side, share not as a private person but with a permission of your institution or university.

column2: Will pulseq have the GUI feature in the future?
column5: Good and important question. There's ongoing work to integrate Pulseq sequences with the existing vendor UI, e.g., Matteo will talk about one such approach tomorrow.

So you can try, but you may need to debug the code a bit.

column5: https://github.com/ismrmrd/siemens_to_ismrmrd

column2: Hi all! How to convert Siemens Raw data .dat to ISMRM raw data .MRD ?
column5: if the .dat data has all necessary MDH counters and flags, e.g. the raw data from a Siemens product sequence, you can use siemens_to_ismrmrd software. If the .dat data is from a Pulseq sequence without any MDH counters etc., you would need to manually label the raw data with all necessary information before MRD conversion.

column2: Hi all! How to convert Siemens Raw data .dat to ISMRM raw data .MRD ?
column5: BTW for GE, we're trying to decide on a simple workable solution (this is in the context of the OpenMOLLI challenge), stay tuned. The approach will likely be to populate most of the header with information from the .seq file, and only extract the raw data (and very minimal site/scanner info) from the raw data file (ScanArchive). Perhaps this should be the model across vendors. Anyone interested is happy to help test, or just stay tuned :)

column2: What important gaps remain between MATLAB Pulseq and PyPulseq? I would like to switch from MATLAB to Python but not sure what I would be giving up, if anything
column5: The Pulseq developer team in Freiburg mainly work in Matlab. We release bugfixes, new features, performance improvement, etc., online for the pulseq matlab toolbox. The PyPulseq version may lag behind a bit. But there are many volunteers in the community to update the PyPulseq from time to time. So I think there should not be much to loss.

column2: Why are image lines outside of ACS set to IMA = false? Is there some rule of thumb here?
column5: IMA basically markes the lines that belong to the ACS region, but would otherwise have also been acquired, if there was no ACS region and only accelerated data were acquired.

column2: is it possible to use an “external” ACS scan? e.g. a rapid flash acquisition prior to the mprage, and still be able use ICE?
column5: yes, we did external ACS for EPI GRAPPA+Partial Fourier. But the current interpreter doesn’t have this feature yet.

column2: I guess the even/odd requirment for IMA changes depending on the value of R?
column5: yes, this was GRAPPA2-case, otherwise IMA labels the lines within the ACS region that would othervise have belonged to the pure accelerated scan without the ACS lines. In other words, the lines, which are only labeled with REF are pure reference lines, whereas the ones which have both REF and 'IMA on simultaneously  belong both to the original accelerated acquisition pattern and the reference data region

column2: Is gadgetron online recon only available for 2D? Is 3D possible?

column2: Can you expand on the difference in the setup for integrated vs separate ref scans. Thx
column5: currently we provde more or less no support for separate reference data acquisition because the integration with the integrated recon is way more compleq. @Qingping Chen had some success implementing EPI with GRAPPA, but it was really sensitive to small changes in the protocol, so we didn't release it up to now

column2: To be clear the automatic reconstruction (with the .seq and mrd files) is possible or no for compressed sensing? Would that recon have to be manual/offline?
column5: We didn’t try CS recon before. So we are not sure if Gadgetron is capable for CS + non-Cartesian with Pulseq. Please check online for this information. But I am pretty sure Gadgetron has the CS algorithms.

column2: Have you also implemented adaptive combine mode?
column5: In the offline recon you are free to use any open source adaptive combine imlementation. In the Matlab toolbox we don't provide any example for that though. On the scanner stored sensitivities are used for the optimal combination, so your sequence has to provide acurate FOV/slice package information. If the geometric ordering of the reconstructed images on the scanner is correct, adaptve combine typically works properly. If however the scanner doesn't know the geometry of your acquied data, then you often see artifacts (black lines within homogeneous signal regions) or suboptimal SNR

column2: Do the labels work similarly for pypulseq?
column5: The acquisition time should be included independent of the use of labels. Not setting labels in (Py)Pulseq simply means that with each readout, the LIN label is increased by 1 and all other labels (like ECO for echo, SLC for slice) are set to 0.

column2: Is it possible to reconstruct non-cartesian trajectories with ICE?
column5: ICE is the Siemens Image Recon Environment on the Siemens Scanners. As far as I know, Siemens doesn't have a default ICE program for any non-Cartisian trajectory (which by itself is a pretty broad term), I might be wrong, but you can build one for your specific traj based on ICE.

column2: Is it possible to reconstruct non-cartesian trajectories with ICE?

column2: Sorry—unrelated question: will MrZero handle the line indexing and reconstruct the image in the correct order, or do I need to sort it myself?
column5: the simulated signal produced by MRzero follows the order of the ADC in the sequence. So if your ADCs are not in order, e.g., an centric ordering of kspace, you would need to somewhat resort the simulated signal by yourserlf.
